fund.name: Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund - Direct Plan
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: This is the core of your plan — a Nifty Midcap 150 index fund at just 0.20% yearly fee. Instead of paying a fund manager to pick stocks, this fund simply owns the 150 biggest mid-size companies in India all at once. Mid-size companies tend to grow faster than large ones over long periods. With 30 years ahead of you, this is where your wealth engine sits. The ultra-low fee means more money compounds in your pocket, not the fund house's.

fund.name: UTI Nifty 500 Value 50 Index Fund - Direct Plan - Growth Option

allocations.rationale: This index fund picks the 50 most 'value-priced' stocks from India's top 500 companies — meaning stocks that are cheaper relative to what the company actually earns. Think of it as buying stocks on sale. It complements your midcap fund nicely — different companies, different style, same ultra-low 0.20% yearly fee. UTI is a completely different fund house from Motilal, so you're properly diversified across companies too.

fund.name: ICICI Prudential Equity & Debt Fund - Direct Plan - Growth
fund.fund_house: ICICI Prudential Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: This fund keeps roughly 75% in stocks and 25% in safer bonds — all in one product. It's your shock absorber. When stock markets fall sharply (which WILL happen), this part of your portfolio won't fall as hard. It's also equity-taxed (just like the index funds), so no extra tax penalty. ICICI Prudential is one of India's largest and most reliable fund houses, and with ₹12,500 crore in this fund, it's a very stable choice. This also doubles as your marriage savings fund — you can gradually shift money here as your 6-year goal approaches.

plan.setup_phase:
**Step 1 — Open a free direct mutual fund account**
Download Kuvera (kuvera.in) or Groww on your phone. These are free platforms. Always choose "Direct Plan" and "Growth Option" when selecting funds — this ensures zero middleman commissions and your money grows instead of being paid out.

**Step 2 — Complete your KYC (one-time, takes 10 minutes)**
Keep your Aadhaar, PAN card, and a selfie ready. It's fully online.

**Step 3 — Park your existing ₹50,000 as lumpsum**
Split it equally across the 3 funds: ₹25,000 → Motilal Nifty Midcap 150, ₹12,500 → UTI Nifty 500 Value 50, ₹12,500 → ICICI Prudential Equity & Debt Fund. Do this first — time in the market starts now.

**Step 4 — Set up your monthly SIPs (auto-debit)**
₹4,000/month → Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund
₹2,000/month → UTI Nifty 500 Value 50 Index Fund
₹2,000/month → ICICI Prudential Equity & Debt Fund
Total: ₹8,000/month. Set SIP date to 1st or 5th of each month, just after your salary credit.

**Step 5 — Emergency fund first (separate from this plan)**
Before or alongside starting SIPs, build your emergency fund of 3 months' salary in a simple savings account or a liquid fund. Don't touch your mutual fund SIPs for emergencies — that's what the emergency fund is for. Target: ₹15,000–₹25,000 saved in a high-interest savings account (like AU Small Finance Bank or IDFC First) paying 6-7%.

**Step 6 — Increase your SIP by 10% every year**
In January each year, log into Kuvera/Groww and increase each SIP by 10%. This is called "step-up SIP." Even small increases make a massive difference over 30 years.
review_checkpoints: After 3 months: Check that all 3 SIPs are running without any failures. Login to Kuvera/Groww and verify the monthly auto-debits are happening. Nothing else to do.
After 6 months: Check if your emergency fund (3 months salary) is fully built in your savings account. If not, pause the UTI fund SIP temporarily and redirect ₹2,000/month to savings until it's done.

Every January (annual review): Step up each SIP by 10%. Takes 5 minutes on the app. Also check if any fund has badly underperformed its category for 2 straight years — but don't panic over short-term dips.

At Year 3 (age 26): You're halfway to your marriage goal (Year 6). Check if the ICICI Hybrid fund portion is on track for ₹5L. If markets have done well, consider parking the marriage fund amount in a liquid fund or FD to protect it from stock market swings.

At Year 5 (age 28, 1 year before marriage target): Move the marriage savings (the ₹5L portion) OUT of equity funds into a safe FD or liquid fund. Never keep money you need in 12 months in the stock market.
Every 3 years: Check if your overall split is roughly 75% equity / 25% hybrid. If one fund has grown much more than others, rebalance by directing new SIPs slightly more toward the laggard.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines: Check your portfolio mix once a year — ideally every January. Your target is roughly 75% in the two index funds and 25% in the hybrid fund. If markets boom and your index funds grow to 85% of your portfolio, redirect SIP top-ups toward the hybrid fund to get back to balance. Never sell existing units to rebalance (triggers taxes and interrupts compounding) — just adjust where future SIPs flow. For your marriage goal specifically: 12 months before the wedding, move that portion completely out of all equity funds into a bank FD. Equity markets can drop 30% in a bad year and you can't wait for recovery when you have a fixed deadline.
plan.rationale:
Katniss, you're 23 with zero debt, a 30-year runway, and every rupee counts. That's actually a fantastic starting position — time is your biggest wealth-building tool, and you have more of it than almost anyone.

Here's the philosophy behind this plan:

**Keep it simple — 3 funds, done.** You don't need 10 funds. You need low-cost, broad diversification. Two ultra-cheap index funds (0.20% yearly fee each) covering hundreds of Indian companies, plus one hybrid fund as your cushion. That's it.

**Low fees are your superpower.** The two index funds charge just 0.20%/year versus 1-2% for most active funds. On ₹8,000/month over 30 years, the fee savings alone could add ₹15–20 lakhs to your final corpus. This is money that stays with you.

**The hybrid fund is your emotional anchor AND your marriage savings vehicle.** When markets fall 30% in a bad year (and they will, at some point), your index funds will fall hard. The hybrid fund softens the blow — it holds bonds too, so it won't fall as much. This helps you NOT panic-sell at the wrong time, which is the #1 mistake investors make. It's also equity-taxed, so no tax disadvantage.

**Tax note:** You're in the new tax regime, so Section 80C ELSS deduction doesn't benefit you as much. All three funds chosen are equity-taxed, meaning only 12.5% tax on long-term gains above ₹1.25 lakh per year — very efficient for a long-term investor.

**The numbers:** Starting with ₹50,000 lumpsum + ₹8,000/month SIP, stepping up 10% annually, at a base 12.5% return over 30 years — you're looking at a corpus of approximately **₹5–6 crore**. Even in a bad scenario (8.5%), you'd cross **₹2.5 crore**. Your ₹5 lakh marriage goal is very achievable in 6 years with the hybrid fund growing at ~12% annually.
risks: Stock markets can drop 20-30% in a bad year. Your portfolio value on the screen will go down. This is completely normal and has happened many times in history — but it always recovered and reached new highs eventually. The key is to NOT sell in a panic and to keep your SIPs running through downturns (you're actually buying more units cheaply when markets fall).

The midcap index fund is more bumpy than a large-cap fund. Mid-size companies can fall harder in crashes. This is the trade-off for higher long-term growth. Since you have 30 years, you have time to ride out these swings.

Your marriage goal in 6 years has a hard deadline — make sure to move that money to safety 1 year before you need it, as described in the review checkpoints. Don't leave it in equity funds right before the wedding.
Inflation is a hidden risk — ₹5 lakh today may buy less in 6 years. Plan for this by targeting ₹6-7 lakh for marriage expenses to be safe.

Life changes — salary, family responsibilities, sending money home — may make it hard to keep the SIP going. Even if you have to drop the SIP amount temporarily, never stop it completely. ₹1,000/month is better than ₹0.
plan.disclaimer: This plan is for educational and research purposes only. It is not certified financial advice. Please consult a SEBI-registered investment advisor before making investment decisions.

sip_step_up.description: Increase each SIP by 10% every January. So in Year 2, your total SIP becomes ₹8,800/month, in Year 3 ₹9,680/month, and so on. This matches typical salary growth and dramatically boosts your final corpus — a ₹8,000 SIP stepping up 10% annually creates roughly 2x the wealth of a flat ₹8,000 SIP over 30 years.

allocation_schedule.trigger: Starting allocation — 75% index equity + 25% aggressive hybrid
Age 28: Move marriage savings (₹5L) to FD/liquid fund 1 year before wedding. Slightly dial back equity.
Age 33: Begin gradual shift — add some gold and safer options as responsibilities grow
Age 43: Midpoint of wealth journey — start slowly moving toward stability
Age 53: 10 years from retirement — steadily reduce stock exposure to protect what you've built

aps.reasoning:
This is a fundamentally passive, low-cost, long-term wealth-building plan with excellent execution discipline.

**Passive Instrument Fraction (0.80):** The plan allocates 50% + 25% = 75% directly to explicit index funds (Nifty Midcap 150 and Nifty 500 Value 50, both at 0.20% ER). The remaining 25% goes to an actively managed aggressive hybrid fund. Treating the hybrid's ~75% equity content, total equity exposure is ~94%, of which ~80% is passive index-tracked. The portfolio is predominantly passive.

**Turnover Score (0.85):** The plan embodies buy-and-hold discipline with minimal rebalancing. Annual reviews occur only in January; rebalancing is achieved via SIP direction (never via selling). Step-up SIPs occur once yearly. The guidance emphasizes riding out market swings without panic-selling. This is low-turnover, patient investing — only minor deductions for annual checkpoints and the marriage fund repositioning 1 year before the goal.

**Cost Emphasis Score (0.88):** Cost minimization is the dominant selection criterion and is explicitly quantified. The plan opens with "Low fees are your superpower" and models the impact of 0.20% vs. 1–2% fees, projecting ₹15–20 lakh in savings. Direct Plan instructions ensure zero intermediation. Specific ER comparisons guide fund selection. The hybrid fund's 1.0% ER reflects a necessary trade-off for defensive positioning, not cost compromise.

**Research vs. Cost Score (0.87):** Zero emphasis on individual security analysis, manager track records, company fundamentals, or alpha generation. The plan explicitly rejects active stock-picking ("Instead of paying a fund manager to pick stocks, this fund simply owns the 150 biggest mid-size companies"). Reliance is entirely on broad-market index methodologies and diversification. The plan advocates holding indices through market cycles without tactical tilts.

**Time Horizon Alignment Score (0.90):** A crystal-clear 30-year investment horizon with explicit recognition that "time is your biggest wealth-building tool." The allocation schedule spans decades with gradual, age-appropriate shifts. The strategy is designed for decades of compounding, not event-driven moves. Annual reviews are patient check-ins, not tactical opportunities. The only fixed deadline (6-year marriage goal) is explicitly de-risked via a predetermined 1-year shift to safety — demonstrating respect for time horizons.

**Portfolio Activeness Score (0.78):** The two index funds (75% of equity allocation) are genuinely passive with near-zero tracking error, beta ~1.0, and zero alpha by design. The 25% hybrid component is actively managed (ICICI Prudential Equity & Debt, 1.0% ER), but the plan describes it functionally as a "shock absorber" and defensive tool, not as an alpha-seeking conviction vehicle. Portfolio-weighted activeness: 75% of funds are index-trackers; 25% is active but defensively positioned. Not a pure passive portfolio, but genuinely more passive than active.

**Overall Profile:** This plan occupies the **strongly passive end** of the APS spectrum. It prioritizes low costs, broad diversification, and buy-and-hold discipline over security-picking or tactical timing. The 30-year horizon, explicit fee emphasis, and reliance on indices are hallmarks of index-aligned passive investing. The one active component (hybrid fund) serves a specific risk-management role, not return-generation ambition. High execution quality: direct plans, systematic SIP step-ups, clear guardrails for tactical rebalancing only via new inflows. This is textbook passive-index-friendly planning for a young investor with a long runway.

pqs.reasoning:
**Goal Alignment (0.90):** The plan excellently addresses all three stated goals. (1) Emergency fund: explicitly separated from investment (Step 5), with clear ₹15-25k target in high-interest savings. (2) Marriage goal (₹5L in 6 years): allocated to ICICI Aggressive Hybrid (25% of portfolio) with realistic 12% projected return, plus CRITICAL protective measure to move marriage corpus to FD 12 months before deadline, preventing equity risk near hard deadline. (3) Long-term wealth: 75% equity (Nifty Midcap 150 + Nifty 500 Value 50) with 30-year runway, 10% annual SIP step-up. Minor deduction: marriage target of ₹5-6L (accounting for inflation) is somewhat ambitious relative to ₹12.5k lumpsum + ₹2k/month without step-up, though protective mechanisms and inflation awareness mitigate this.

**Diversification (0.85):** Good diversification across multiple dimensions. Asset class: 75% equity / 25% hybrid balance. Within equity: two complementary index funds (midcap growth + large-cap value style) covering ~150-500 companies across all Indian sectors. Fund houses: Motilal Oswal, UTI, ICICI Prudential (three independent providers). No concentration risk. Gap: Zero international exposure (acceptable given investor's stage, frugal lifestyle, ₹8k/month budget, and stated preference for simplicity; noted as opportunity for future enhancement but not critical).

**Risk-Return Appropriateness (0.88):** Risk profile matches moderate appetite well. The 75% equity + 25% Aggressive Hybrid (which itself is 75% equity) creates ~81% blended equity exposure with volatility cushioning, appropriate for 30-year horizon. Return projections are realistic: base 12.5% (aligned with historical Nifty/midcap long-term returns), bull 16.5%, bear 8.5%. Corpus projection of ₹5-6 crore at base case and ₹2.5+ crore at bear case are credible. Plan explicitly educates on 20-30% market drops and addresses panic-selling (the #1 mistake), which is critical for a young investor. Minor deduction: bear case (8.5%) at the low end of typical ranges; projections could have been more granular on time-weighted average returns given the SIP step-up feature.

**Internal Consistency (0.95):** Exceptional coherence throughout. Rationale ("keep it simple, 3 funds, low fees") matches exactly: 3 funds chosen, two at 0.20%, one at 1.0%. All SIP amounts (₹4k + ₹2k + ₹2k = ₹8k/month) and lumpsum split (₹25k + ₹12.5k + ₹12.5k = ₹50k) add up correctly. Tax narrative is internally consistent: correctly states new regime investor cannot claim 80C, then avoids ELSS recommendation (correct decision) and chooses all three funds as equity-taxed instruments. Rebalancing guidelines (target 75/25, adjust via SIP flows, never sell for rebalancing) are consistent with the simplicity mandate. Risk warnings align with strategy (acknowledge drops, encourage SIPs through downturns). Allocation schedule evolution (2025–2055) follows logical lifecycle progression: 75% equity → gradually 50% equity over 30 years, appropriate for moving from age 23 to 53. Minor deduction: the post-2030 allocation schedule is speculative for a 23-year-old (10+ year decisions premature).

**Tax Efficiency (0.92):** Outstanding tax optimization for new regime investor. (1) Correctly reads tax_bracket="new_regime" and avoids recommending ELSS (which new regime cannot claim 80C for—common mistake avoided). (2) All three funds selected are equity-taxed: Nifty Midcap 150 (100% equity), Nifty 500 Value 50 (100% equity), ICICI Equity & Debt (75% equity, ≥65% threshold = equity-taxed as Aggressive Hybrid). (3) No slab-taxed debt/liquid/overnight funds are recommended—this avoids the trap of a high-slab investor (though investor is new regime, the principle of avoiding slab-taxed instruments where better alternatives exist is sound). (4) Plan explicitly states tax benefit: "12.5% tax on long-term gains above ₹1.25 lakh per year." (5) Marriage fund housed in equity-taxed Aggressive Hybrid ensures gains locked at favorable LTCG rate when moved to FD at year 5. Minor deduction: Could have been more explicit about the ₹1.25L annual LTCG exemption threshold and whether investor might cross it; could have mentioned that new regime simplifies overall tax calculation beyond just MF taxation; no discussion of complementary deductions (rent, NPS, etc.) outside MF scope.

**Plan Strengths:** Exceptional simplicity and clarity for a first-job investor. Step-by-step setup instructions (Kuvera/Groww, KYC, direct plan emphasis) eliminate barriers. SIP automation + annual step-up (10%) matches salary growth. Review checkpoints are practical and actionable. Realistic projected corpus (₹5-6 crore base case) builds confidence. Emergency fund guidance is essential and explicit. Marriage goal protection via FD at year 5 shows mature financial planning. Tone is encouraging without overconfidence.

**Plan Weaknesses:** No international diversification (acceptable but could note as future enhancement). Marriage corpus ₹5-6L target feels optimistic for ₹12.5k lumpsum + ₹2k/month mix without step-up on that fund, though inflation considerations and protective measures mitigate. Allocation schedule 2035+ is premature for a 23-year-old.
